mcqs.question: In C++, which access specifier ensures maximum encapsulation by restricting member access to the class itself only?

options.text: public
protected
private
default

mcqs.explanation: The private access specifier restricts member access to within the class only, providing the highest level of data hiding.

mcqs.question: When is the default constructor automatically provided by the compiler in C++?

options.text: When no constructor is defined in the class
Only when all data members are public
Only when the class has no destructor
Only for classes with primitive data types

mcqs.explanation: The compiler generates a default constructor when no user-defined constructor exists, enabling object creation without arguments.

mcqs.question: Consider a class 'Employee' with a static int member 'count'. If we create three objects of Employee, how many separate memory locations exist for 'count'?

options.text: Three (one per object)
Zero (stored in code segment)
One (shared by all objects)
Depends on the compiler optimization

mcqs.explanation: Static data members exist in a single memory location shared among all objects of the class, regardless of object count.

mcqs.question: Which statement is true about const member functions in C++?

options.text: They can modify static data members
They can call non-const member functions
They guarantee not to modify any data member of the object
They cannot be invoked using const objects

mcqs.explanation: Const member functions promise not to modify the object's state, ensuring read-only access and enabling calls on const objects.

mcqs.question: In C++, why must initializer lists be used for initializing a reference member and a const data member?

options.text: It improves execution speed slightly
References and const members cannot be initialized in the constructor body and must be initialized at the point of declaration
The compiler requires it for all data members
It prevents memory leaks

mcqs.explanation: References and const members must be initialized when created and cannot be assigned later, making initializer lists mandatory.

mcqs.question: For a class containing a pointer to dynamically allocated memory, what is the key difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy performed by the copy constructor?

options.text: Shallow copy is faster than deep copy
Shallow copy duplicates the pointer, while deep copy allocates new memory and copies the actual data
Deep copy is always called by default
There is no functional difference

mcqs.explanation: Deep copy creates independent memory for dynamically allocated data, preventing aliasing issues that occur with shallow copy's shared pointers.

mcqs.question: When overloading the binary '+' operator as a member function in a class 'Vector', which syntax correctly implements it?

options.text: Vector operator+(const Vector& other) const
Vector operator+(const Vector& other)
void operator+(const Vector& other)
friend Vector operator+(const Vector& a, const Vector& b)

mcqs.explanation: When implemented as a member function, the binary operator takes one parameter (the right operand) and typically returns a new object, often declared const.

mcqs.question: In C++, a friend function of a class:

options.text: Has access to all members but is not part of the class
Must be called using an object of the class
Can access private and protected members without being a member function
Cannot be used for operator overloading

mcqs.explanation: Friend functions are non-member functions granted special access to private and protected members, commonly used for operator overloading.
